message:
This piece was inspired by a Facebook page called OPTIC Politics. They posted: “Analyze the political psychology surrounding Toby Tiangco's statements.” I was like, “Sure. Challenge accepted.”

But the more I read through Tiangco’s words, the less it felt like calm analysis and the more it felt like The Lion King. The man was doing a Mufasa on top of the rock holding Sara aloft, talking about fear, destiny, and “let the people decide” while the rest of us live with the stampede, hoping nobody would notice it.

I was just studying my blog's analytics and I saw that a good number of you reading www.morningcoffeethoughts.org are from Quezon City, Manila, Taguig, Cebu City, and Pasig — and that's just the top five.

Thank you for reading. It means a lot that you keep coming back.

— Gino, MCT

Short commentary lang before I go to bed: 

While writing the COMELEC and Rodante Marcoleta piece, I just realized that COMELEC’s logo carries the Latin phrase “Vox Populi, Vox Dei” — “the voice of the people is the voice of God.”

Ang irony nito? The line is traced back to Alcuin of York, adviser to Charlemagne, who warned rulers not to listen to those who keep saying “vox populi, vox Dei,” because “the riotousness of the crowd is always very close to madness.”

Later on, English preachers, Whig pamphleteers (a British political faction), and eventually modern democracies repurposed it into a feel‑good slogan about popular sovereignty and people power, quietly airbrushing Alcuin’s original warning about mob rule.

Alcuin of York must be turning over in his grave seeing his warning turned into a marketing line for elections.

Which makes me more wary of COMELEC now that I realize they are literally wearing, on their official seal, a quote that was originally a caution against blindly treating the crowd’s noise as if it were divine will.

Curious lang: Didn’t anyone in COMELEC bother to research this when they put the logo together in 1940? 

I knew this information when I was 16. A room full of lawyers and technocrats shouldn’t find that too difficult to research, should they? 🤷🏻

Good night.

COMELEC cleared Rodante Marcoleta over his SOCE issues, but the way they did it may have just set a dangerous precedent for future elections.

If one senator can walk away on technicalities while his big donors get pushed toward the BIR, we need to start asking harder questions about who the rules really protect—and keep watching what COMELEC does next.

Going out to run multiple errands today.

I decided to wear my Facts First shirt bilang suporta sa advocacy ni Sir Christian.

Isabay ko na rin ang panawagan na suportahan po natin ang ating mga good governance advocates — marami po sila.

You can mention their names in the comment section. Ayaw ko lang magbanggit para wala akong makalimutan, but please help me spread the word.

You see, mahirap ang ginagawa nila. They use their own money for what they do every day. 

Sure, some of them are monetized, pero ang totoo, hindi sila mga influencer na malaki ang kita, or tulad ng mga DDS vloggers na may malaking financial backer.

Yung monetization sa social media? Barya lang po iyon — unless you have millions of followers.

So, if you see that they have merch, please buy directly from them.

Support po natin sila. Salamat po.

It took me several days to research this article—if you scroll down to the source list, you’ll understand why.

Listening to Lacson and Marcoleta argue about the Kalayaan Group of Islands, I realized something uncomfortable: I actually had no idea how these islands became “ours” in the first place.

So this blog isn’t a final verdict or a patriotic speech. It’s my attempt, as one Filipino reader, to understand the real story of the Kalayaan Group of Islands and what we’re actually standing on when we say, “ atin ’yan.”

If you have time (and patience), I hope you’ll read it—and tell me where I got it wrong, or what I missed.

I agree with what Chris Tan said - the COMELEC didn't just make a decision to save Marcoleta, they effectively lawyered for him. I haven't written about this yet because gigil na gigil ako sa mga hinayupak na to. I need to calm down before I can write or I am risking my own health.

Anong ginagawa ni Paolo Marcoleta? Hindi ba to nagbabasa ng rules bago siya sumabak at magtanong? Or is he wasting  the time of the justice committee? 

I get na hindi naman masama magtanong, pero kung yung sagot nasa rule book na hindi mo binasa tapos magtatanong ka, magmumukha ka lang tanga.

His question reminds me of the typical classroom scene:

Teacher: Class, may quiz tayo. Prepare a 1/2 sheet of paper. Lengthwise.

Some student at  the back: Teacher, 1/2 po?

Another student: Teacher, crosswise po ba o lengthwise?

🤷🏽‍♂️

Thank you so much po for sending your GCash donation. Your generosity means a lot and directly helps me continue this work. 

Every amount you send is a real sacrifice, and I don’t take that lightly. I see your support, I feel it, and I’m truly grateful. 

From the bottom of my heart, maraming salamat sa tiwala at suporta. You’re part of this journey with me, and I’ll do my best to honor that.

Spain’s king just admitted Spain committed “abuses” as a colonial power—more than 300 years too late for Rizal and the millions who paid with blood, land, and dignity.  

Imagine Rizal hearing this from the same crown that shot him in Bagumbayan: proof that our heroes were right all along, yet still no full apology, no justice, no repair—only words trying to catch up with the truth they died telling.

Prices are going up and honestly I don't think it's stopping anytime soon. Oil, electricity, food — it's all connected to what's happening globally right now.

So I want to ask the community — what are you doing to cope? And more importantly, how are you helping the people around you?

It doesn't have to be big. Buying from your neighbor's small business instead of the mall. Carpooling. Sharing price tips in the market. Letting someone know na may mas mura dito o doon.

What's working for you? Drop it in the comments. Baka makatulong sa iba.

A quick update about the MCT Android app and the Hub.

A lot of you emailed asking if you can try the Android app and help test it. Some of you already tested it and even sent full PDF reports with screenshots and notes. I see them. Thank you for doing that.

Some also asked why https://hub.morningcoffeethoughts.org is not updated, even if there are many new posts on the main blog. You’re right, it’s not.

For now, the Android app and the Hub are on hold. MCT is crowdfunded, and right now I just don’t have the budget to keep those two running.

I don’t want every post to turn into a fundraising story. So I’ll just say this: the app and the Hub are paused while I figure out a more stable setup. The main blog and this page will continue. I’ll still be here writing and posting.

Thank you to everyone who tested, emailed, asked, or just quietly reads.

Good job and thank you. Pero question lang: bakit may mukha? Can someone explain this to me? Why is the face necessary? Wasn't there a recent order na tanggalin ang pagmumukha ng mga opisyal ng gobyerno sa mga events and projects na ganito? So, exempted ang DSWD? Paki-explain. Love you.

I’ve been looking back at the past surveys — trust and satisfaction ratings versus how many people actually want Sara Duterte impeached — and, to be honest, ang labo. On paper, she’s still one of the most trusted officials, but at the same time, a big part of the public also wants her to face an impeachment trial and answer every allegation.

This blog is me trying to make sense of that seeming disconnect — not as an expert or analyst, just someone with a cup of coffee trying to figure out what’s really going on.

Turned my notes about the Independent Commission for Infrastructure into a full blog so I can revisit the details later—numbers, names, and what actually happened before it shut down. 

It’s a bit long because there’s way too much data, but I tried my best to compress it and hopefully didn’t miss anything important. 

Sharing it in case you’re also trying to make sense of what really went on with our flood control money.

Good morning sa lahat ng owners ng e-trikes, e-bikes, e-kick scooters, at electric cars.  

Sabi nila, ang laki ng natitipid niyo, especially now na sobrang taas ng presyo ng krudo.  

But I want to ask: kamusta ang experience mo as an owner of an electric vehicle? Malaki ba talaga ang natitipid? Sulit ba? What unit do you own? Kamusta ang maintenance? Any negative experience so far?  

Please share them in the comments. I’m really curious how it is in real life, hindi lang sa ads at kwento-kwento.  

Personally, I’ve been seriously contemplating getting an electric vehicle for errands lang. Aside from saving money, it also helps the environment because it has lower emissions compared to traditional vehicles.  

If you were in my place—based on your own experience—would you say: go for it, or huwag muna?

Morning Coffee Thoughts 2.0 just hit 50,000 strong.  

When I started sharing random “kape at politika” musings, I never imagined this many people would pull up a chair to the table. 

Every comment, react, and quiet “nakakarelate ako dito” kept this little corner of the internet alive.  

This community proved that ordinary people—with coffee in hand and questions in mind—can talk about power, policy, and the future of the country without needing a title in front of their name. 

That’s always been the heart of MCT: kwentuhan, kritikal na pag-iisip, at malasakit.  

To the OGs, the silent readers, the sharers, and even the kontrarians: salamat.

Let’s make MCT 2.0 braver, kinder, and louder—mas maraming tanong, mas maraming kwento, mas maraming katotohanang hindi natatakot sabihin.  

Here’s to the next cup, the next post, and the next 50,000.

Maraming salamat po at magandang gabi.

Marcos once voted to join the ICC. Now he calls it a “threat to sovereignty” while quietly benefiting from Duterte’s trial in The Hague.

In this piece on Marcos, Duterte, and the ICC, I trace how a president can reject a court in public, use its power in private, and still claim our justice system is “good enough” for thousands of drug war deaths.

Good morning. Sabi ko kahapon I will write a piece on Marcos and his refusal to rejoin ICC and another piece on Guanzon's 2028 plan. Tapos na ang article, but I'm not happy with the output, so I kept revising it. I will post it later.

This is my weakness — when an idea hits my head, I have a hard time letting it go. The only way I can get rid of it is by writing it down. I'm not sure if I'm the only one like that, but it's partly why I blog.

Anyway, kape muna tayo. This morning, I made a  cup of ristretto bianco (a short, intense espresso pulled with less water, topped with steamed milk). I need something stronger so I can power through several articles.

Maganda ka pa sa umaga.

I’ve been reading the comments section, and it shocks me to my core when some of us use the phrase, "don’t judge people," especially when talking about politicians and government officials.

I don’t know how to tell you this, so I’ll say it straight: politicians and government officials are there for you to judge.

You’re supposed to. It’s your job. It is your responsibility as a voter and as a Filipino.

The present and future of the Philippines are in our hands, and if we fail at this small but significant task, we are literally harming the country.

Our failure to judge politicians is the very reason why violent narcissists end up in the highest offices, why the unqualified end up as vice president, why there are traitors in the Senate, why Congress is a den of thieves, and why political dynasties are so hard to break.

No one is coming to fix this for us; the country only changes when we finally take our power as voters seriously.

Judge government officials and politicians—every single time. Your country will thank you for it.

As a Catholic and an ex‑seminarian, I’m really struggling with this.

How do I reconcile someone who kneels in church, talks about mercy and faith, and at the same time publicly tells stories about kill orders and threats?

I know the Church keeps its doors open. We don’t turn people away from mass. I built Morning Coffee Thoughts to try to understand things like this, but with this one, I need help.

So I’m asking for advice from a priest. Any priest. How should I hold this?

Dumaan sa newsfeed ko yung account ni Lorraine Badoy. 

The post was a video of Ronald Llamas walking. 

The objective of the post? So her followers can laugh at Llamas. 

Normal ba sa kanya yung mga ganoong post? 

I ask because from what I know about her, she is educated. But even that can’t protect her from becoming a disgusting pig. 

Wala siyang binatbat kay Llamas, so all she could post about him is his manner of walking. 

Basta talaga nahawakan ka ni Duterte, masisira ka. Such is the Duterte touch; it destroys even the educated ones. 

Now imagine a Philippines with people like Badoy at the helm. We become a pigsty. Again.

Sabi ko sana magpapahinga ako today from writing political blogs. Oh well.

Makapaglaba na nga lang muna.

The wicked does not rest — why should we?

Buti na lang doing laundry is my favorite weekend water sport.

I’m currently writing two pieces — one on Marcos’s decision not to rejoin the ICC, and Guanzon’s senate plan. Papalagpasin ko ba yon? 

Later na though, mag-kape muna tayo.

Magandang umaga. Kung naghahanap ka ng Tagalog version ng mga post ng MCT, please visit https://hub.morningcoffeethoughts.org.

Click any article. Right under the title, you will see a button that says "Basahin sa Filipino."

Please note that this is a machine translation and is sometimes not accurate. But it does the job, ang tanong na lang is kaya mo bang magbasa ng mahahabang blogs para mas maunawaan mo ang sitwasyon sa bansa at makapag desisyon ka ng tama para sa kinabukasan ng Pilipinas? 

Salamat. Kape tayo.

An Open Letter to the Integrated Bar of the Philippines

Dear IBP,

Your Human Rights Committee released a statement during Women's Month condemning misogyny and sexism in the legal profession. It said, in effect, that there is "no room" for it in the noble profession. 

Fine. But a statement is one thing. Doing something about it is another.

You have the power to initiate motu proprio proceedings against erring members. Your own president, Atty. Panolong, confirmed this. 

Under Rule 139-B of the Rules of Court, the IBP Board of Governors can investigate and prosecute lawyers whose conduct is unbecoming, even without a formal complaint from a victim. 

You don't need to wait. You don't need permission. You have the mechanism. 

Use it.

We already saw this work before. Larry Gadon got disbarred — unanimously, 15-0 — by the Supreme Court for his profane, sexist tirade against journalist Raissa Robles. 

The SC used the exact same language you are now echoing: "There is no room in this noble profession for misogyny and sexism." 

So the precedent is there. The words have already been said. What is needed now is action, not a recycled quote on Facebook.

Rep. Sarah Elago has already filed a disbarment complaint against Topacio before your organization. 

Akbayan Youth has called for it. 

The Philippine Bar Association is calling on you and the Supreme Court to hold lawyers accountable. 

Even the Philippine Commission on Women laid out the full legal basis — the Safe Spaces Act, the Magna Carta of Women, RA 6713. 

Everyone is doing their part. The question is: will you do yours, or will you wait until public pressure forces your hand — again?

Police your ranks. 

Otherwise, you are just a big boys' club — no different from the Philippine Senate, where colleagues shield each other from consequences and ethics complaints go nowhere. 

At least the House of Representatives moved: Suntay faced an ethics complaint quickly after his remarks. 

The Senate? Good luck getting that kind of accountability there. 

If even the lower house — not exactly known for being a bastion of progressive values — can act fast, what is your excuse?

Your organization exists for many reasons. But when one of your members weaponizes a radio broadcast to slut-shame and sexually harass a sitting congresswoman during Women's Month, your silence becomes complicity. 

You have pigs in your ranks. Acknowledge it. Be quick about it — investigate, sanction, recommend disbarment if warranted — because every day you delay, their fault becomes your institutional failure.

You are responsible for them. Act. Do not just speak.

Respectfully but firmly,

A Concerned Citizen

Oboy Alconaba left a comment on yesterday's adversarial fact-checking post that I thought deserved more than just a reply.

He said: "Thanks for the info. Now I am just wondering how can I use logic and critical thinking with what you have said. Can you help me?"

I'm not entirely sure if that was a challenge or a genuine question — and honestly, it doesn't matter, because either way it's the kind of comment I'm always willing to engage with seriously.

So let me answer it properly.

When I say "use logic and critical thinking," here's basically how it works at the personal level: take any claim you see — about Martial Law, the economy, a politician's record — put it in your own words, then actively look for evidence that would prove it wrong instead of searching for posts that confirm what you already believe.

Check fact-checking sites, primary sources, official data, court records.

If the claim still holds up after you've genuinely tried to break it, then it probably deserves your belief. If it falls apart quickly, that's your signal not to share it.

But here's the harder part of Oboy's question, and the one most people skip over: how do we teach this to people at scale?

That's a much more uncomfortable conversation. Because logic, critical thinking, and reasoning don't come naturally to most people — and I mean that without any condescension.

Research in cognitive psychology is very clear on this: even educated adults default to flawed reasoning when they haven't been trained to catch it. Peter Wason's experiments in the 1960s already showed that.

Most people can't distinguish a premise from a conclusion, and they treat emotional appeals and personal stories as valid evidence — not because they're stupid, but because nobody ever taught them otherwise.

And that's the real problem.

Our school system never seriously taught us how to think. It taught us what to think. So when people encounter misinformation, they don't have the internal tools to break it down — and that's a systemic failure, not a personal one.

The fix isn't just sharing good posts — and yes, I'm aware of the irony of saying that on a post.

Sustained, deliberate practice is what actually works: reading carefully, questioning your own assumptions, and being genuinely willing to find out you were wrong. 

That's not a one-comment lesson. It's a habit you build over time, and it requires both education and actual knowledge of the subject you're trying to evaluate.

If you've been following MCT for a while, you'd remember that I've always reminded people to question everything. Question everyone. Question even MCT.

The whole point of that isn't just about me or this page — it's about training your mind to look for truth instead of just looking for agreement. Always doubt what you find online before you do anything with it.

That right there is already the start of critical thinking — not a course, not a seminar, just the simple act of asking questions first before you agree, disagree, or hit share.

And if you've noticed, MCT has taken a different path from most pro-good governance pages and bloggers out there.

No disrespect to my fellow bloggers and vloggers who have been doing this far longer than I have.

A lot of them are fighting fire left, right, and center — and many of them are also educating and addressing the issues at the same time. 

I respect that deeply, because that fight matters and it's twice as hard. But I've also come to see the reactive side of it as one step forward, two steps back.

There are already so many doing it that way. So instead of adding to the traffic, I made a decision early on to try a different approach — educate through facts, through analysis, through research, through critical thinking.

Not just react. Build.

Because if we can help even a handful of people think better, that does more damage to disinformation than a hundred callout posts ever will.

So to answer you directly, Oboy — the framework is in this post. The rest is practice.
